title: Self-harm immediate safety circle
summary: Focused extreme medical support for self-harm immediate safety circle in offline, delayed-help, or collapse conditions.
content: When someone may harm themselves, the first task is not debate; it is reducing immediate danger and connecting them to live help. This is not medical advice; it is a safety and organization checklist until qualified help is available.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “Self-harm immediate safety circle”.
soap or sanitizer. Context: “Self-harm immediate safety circle”.
gloves or clean barrier. Context: “Self-harm immediate safety circle”.
paper and pen. Context: “Self-harm immediate safety circle”.
phone, radio, or messenger — for «Self-harm immediate safety circle»
labels for time and symptoms — for «Self-harm immediate safety circle»
steps: Stay with the person or assign a calm trusted adult if safe.
Move weapons, poisons, ropes, medicines, and other immediate means away without confrontation.
Call emergency services or crisis support.
Use simple statements: you are not alone, help is coming, stay with me.
Do not shame, challenge, or leave them isolated.
warnings: This is not medical advice and it is not a doctor's advice. It is offline emergency organization guidance only. Use local emergency numbers, trained first aid, pharmacists, clinics, doctors, poison centers, and official public-health instructions whenever available. Do not perform surgery, invasive procedures, diagnosis, prescription changes, or medicine dosing from this guide. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Self-harm immediate safety circle”.

Escalate immediately for trouble breathing, heavy bleeding, unconsciousness, severe pain, chest pain, stroke signs, severe burns, pregnancy danger signs, infants in distress, poisoning, or rapid worsening. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Self-harm immediate safety circle”.
Do not delay evacuation or professional care to finish a checklist. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Self-harm immediate safety circle”.
Keep privacy, consent, dignity, and local law in mind even during collapse. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Self-harm immediate safety circle”.
common_mistakes: Trying to diagnose instead of recognizing danger signs while carrying out “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
Forgetting times, medicines, allergies, and changes while carrying out “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
Touching fluids or wounds without hand hygiene while carrying out “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
Moving an injured person without a safety reason while carrying out “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
Letting panic, crowds, or rumors control care while carrying out “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
alternatives: Use certified first-aid training where available as a fallback for “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
Use official emergency services or the nearest clinic as a fallback for “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
Use a pharmacist for medicine questions as a fallback for “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
Use radio/phone consultation if transport is delayed as a fallback for “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
Use written handover notes when devices fail as a fallback for “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
failure_signs: Breathing, bleeding, consciousness, pain, fever, confusion, weakness, or skin color gets worse during “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
The helper cannot keep the scene clean or safe during “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
Transport, water, power, or communication failure blocks care during “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
The person becomes unable to drink, speak, walk, recognize people, or stay awake during “Self-harm immediate safety circle”
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to replace a doctor, ambulance, clinic, pharmacist, poison center, or trained first-aider when using the “Self-harm immediate safety circle” record
Do not use it for surgery or invasive treatment when using the “Self-harm immediate safety circle” record
Do not use it to change prescription medicines or doses when using the “Self-harm immediate safety circle” record
Do not force care on a conscious adult except immediate life-safety actions allowed by local rules when using the “Self-harm immediate safety circle” record
short_term: Reduce immediate danger, call or send for qualified help, keep the person clean, warm or cool as needed, and record facts without guessing. This note applies specifically to Self-harm immediate safety circle.
long_term: Build certified first-aid capacity, update medical records, protect supplies, practice handovers, and map routes to pharmacies, clinics, hospitals, and shelters. This note applies specifically to Self-harm immediate safety circle.
if_method_fails: Escalate by the fastest safe route: emergency number, clinic, hospital, pharmacist, poison center, radio medical consult, trained first-aider, or evacuation convoy. Record what failed and when. This note applies specifically to Self-harm immediate safety circle.
environment_notes: In apartments, shelters, rubble zones, heatwaves, floods, smoke, and outages, medical problems worsen because of crowding, poor air, poor hygiene, no elevator, no refrigeration, and delayed transport. This note applies specifically to Self-harm immediate safety circle.

title: Panic and dissociation grounding in shelter
summary: Focused extreme medical support for panic and dissociation grounding in shelter in offline, delayed-help, or collapse conditions.
content: Crowds, explosions, grief, and sleep loss can trigger panic or dissociation. Practical grounding helps until professional support is available. This is not medical advice; it is a safety and organization checklist until qualified help is available.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “Panic and dissociation grounding in shelter”.
soap or sanitizer. Context: “Panic and dissociation grounding in shelter”.
gloves or clean barrier. Context: “Panic and dissociation grounding in shelter”.
paper and pen. Context: “Panic and dissociation grounding in shelter”.
phone, radio, or messenger — for «Panic and dissociation grounding in shelter»
labels for time and symptoms — for «Panic and dissociation grounding in shelter»
steps: Move to a quieter safer edge of the shelter.
Speak slowly and use the person’s name if known.
Ask them to notice feet on floor, one object they see, one sound, and one slow breath.
Offer water only if fully alert and safe to swallow.
Escalate if chest pain, fainting, self-harm risk, psychosis, or medical symptoms appear.
warnings: This is not medical advice and it is not a doctor's advice. It is offline emergency organization guidance only. Use local emergency numbers, trained first aid, pharmacists, clinics, doctors, poison centers, and official public-health instructions whenever available. Do not perform surgery, invasive procedures, diagnosis, prescription changes, or medicine dosing from this guide. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Panic and dissociation grounding in shelter”.
